session.title: Three workstreams, one day: mining, consolidation, orchestration

nodes.title: Transcript mining
nodes.summary: Mined three weeks of session transcripts for recurring friction, distilled 19 raw patterns into 12 themes, and converted them into an approved 7-item buildout with a dependency-ordered build sequence and paste-ready spec files.

nodes.title: Scoped the mining corpus
nodes.summary: Corpus fixed at 21 days of transcripts (2026-06-13 to 2026-07-03): Claude Code JSONL from the personal machine plus claude.ai chat exports. Work-machine material excluded entirely rather than sanitized — cheaper and safer. Unit of analysis is the friction event (a moment Pi repeats himself, re-explains state, or re-does setup), not the session.

nodes.title: Extracted friction patterns from the corpus
nodes.summary: Two extraction passes over the full corpus (Claude Code JSONL, then claude.ai exports) surfaced 19 raw friction patterns, deduplicated to 12 themes. Heaviest hitters: re-pasting project state at session start (31 occurrences), permission-prompt interruptions on read-only commands (26), manually summarizing a dead session to seed its successor (14).

residuals.label: First tally double-counted
residuals.detail: The first pass reported 23 patterns; the grep that bucketed friction events counted a multi-line match once per line, inflating three buckets. Re-ran with fixed bucketing before anything downstream consumed the numbers.

history.summary: First pass over the Claude Code JSONL only: 14 raw friction patterns, provisionally bucketed into 9 themes. claude.ai exports not yet included.
history.superseded_because: Second pass added the claude.ai chat exports; 5 new patterns appeared (mostly context-rebuilding after web sessions) and the dedup merged the buckets differently — 19 raw patterns, 12 themes.

nodes.title: Approved the 7-item buildout, dependency-ordered
nodes.summary: Pi approved seven build items from the 12 themes (session-start context loader, permission allowlist sweep, session journaling hooks, filesystem watcher, staleness statusline, memory sweep, daily digest). Build sequence is dependency-derived, not numeric: 0, 1, 3, 6, 2, 5, 4 — one fresh session per item, Pi kicks off each one himself.

history.summary: Pi approved the seven items with a plain numeric build order, 0 through 6, on the assumption the items were independent.
history.superseded_because: The dependency pass during orchestration design showed item 3's filesystem watcher is a prerequisite for item 6's digest, and item 2's journaling output feeds item 5's memory sweep. Sequence re-derived from the dependency graph: 0, 1, 3, 6, 2, 5, 4.

quote.text: yes — re-order it. 0 then 1, then 3 before 6, then 2, 5, 4. one session per item, and I kick each one off myself. nothing starts unprompted.

nodes.title: Wrote per-item spec files with kickoff prompts
nodes.summary: Each approved item got its own spec file under a local plans folder: problem statement, transcript evidence (pattern counts and example lines), acceptance criteria, and a paste-ready kickoff prompt so a fresh session can start the item cold. An index README carries the sequence and gate rules.

nodes.title: Re-mine cadence: 30 days, gated on item 2 shipping
nodes.summary: The mining pass repeats in 30 days, but only once item 2 (session journaling hooks) has shipped — item 2 changes what future transcripts contain, so re-mining before it lands would measure the old world.

quote.text: re-run the mining 30 days out, but gate it on item 2 shipping — no point mining transcripts the new hooks haven't touched yet.

nodes.title: File consolidation
nodes.summary: Inventoried six scattered note-and-scratch locations, decided everything folds into the foundry monorepo (no new repo), and executed the first migration batch — 143 files from the two lowest-risk sources. Batch 2 (old-laptop backup, gists) is mapped but not yet moved.

nodes.title: Inventoried the scattered sources
nodes.summary: Six source locations catalogued from Pi's pasted directory listings: ~/Desktop note piles, ~/Downloads keepers, an old 'scratch' repo, an iCloud notes export, an old-laptop backup folder, and a set of GitHub gists. Roughly 400 files total; about a third are duplicates of each other.

nodes.title: session-lens: notes only — no code ever existed
nodes.summary: Two 2025 notes reference a 'session-lens repo' that appears nowhere. Established it was a design sketch that never reached code: the notes predate any build, and 'repo' was aspirational. The only session-lens artifacts are those two markdown files, both already in the consolidation inventory. Nothing is lost; nothing to migrate.

residuals.label: The hunt for a repo that never existed
residuals.detail: Treated 'see session-lens repo' in the notes as a lost repository. Searched GitHub (public and private, plus org memberships), had Pi run find over two machines' dev directories, and grepped an old external-drive backup index he pasted in. All empty. The tell was the note's own date: it predates every dev-directory snapshot, and its sibling note says 'if I ever build this'. Roughly 40 minutes of chat.

nodes.title: Everything folds into the foundry monorepo
nodes.summary: No new repo. Live material goes to the matching foundry subfolder (docs/, research/, skills/), dead material to archived/, personal scratch to the gitignored area. One place to search was the deciding argument.

residuals.label: Rejected: a separate 'attic' archive repo
residuals.detail: A dedicated archive repo was argued for about twenty lines — cleaner history, smaller monorepo. Rejected because a second home reintroduces the exact 'where did I put it' problem the consolidation exists to kill.

quote.text: no new repo. everything folds into foundry — live stuff to the right subfolder, dead stuff to archived/. one place to search.

nodes.title: Mapped every source to its destination
nodes.summary: Produced a source-to-destination table covering all six locations: each file class mapped to a foundry path, duplicates resolved newest-wins, and two risk tiers defined — batch 1 (Desktop piles, scratch repo: no ambiguity) and batch 2 (backup folder, gists: needs dedup review before moving).

nodes.title: Executed batch 1: 143 files moved
nodes.summary: Generated a shell script implementing the batch-1 mapping (moves plus a manifest log); Pi ran it locally and pasted the output back. 143 files landed in foundry, zero collisions, manifest saved alongside. Batch 2 stays parked until its dedup review.

nodes.title: Orchestration design
nodes.summary: Named the failure mode that kills long-running orchestrator sessions (execution debris), ruled that orchestrators decide while workers execute, and drafted a paste-ready mandate template that boots any project's orchestrator session. How orchestrator state survives session death is still open.

nodes.title: Named the failure mode: execution debris
nodes.summary: Walked three recent long sessions that degraded: each one died because tool output, file dumps, and retry noise crowded out the decisions. The diagnosis: sessions that both decide and execute always drown in their own execution. The mining data independently backs this — 'manually summarizing a dead session to seed its successor' was a top-3 friction theme.

nodes.title: Two-tier rule: the orchestrator never executes
nodes.summary: Standing rule for every future project session: the orchestrator holds decisions, state, and gates; all real work is dispatched to subagents or worker sessions that return results. Applies to design work too, not just code.

quote.text: the orchestrator never executes. it holds decisions, state, and gates — everything else gets dispatched and comes back as a result. if the orchestrator's context fills with execution debris we've already lost.

nodes.title: Drafted the orchestrator mandate template
nodes.summary: A paste-ready kickoff block that turns a fresh session into a project orchestrator: two-tier rule stated up front, scaffold list (project home, external state file, tracking issue), phase/gate structure, and a rule that Pi advances gates explicitly. Applied to the buildout specs the same afternoon — each item's kickoff prompt is an instance of it.

nodes.title: How does orchestrator state survive session death?
nodes.summary: The mandate says state lives on disk (a MISSION.md-style file updated every turn), but two things are unresolved: how a successor session verifies the file is current rather than stale, and whether per-turn disk writes are noise in short sessions. Parked deliberately — the cartographer project will hit this first and its answer becomes the pattern.
